speaker: interviewer
text: It's September 30, 1996. We're continuing with the interview of Joyce Lipper. And this is tape number four. So the war had ended. And you were back in your town in Klewan, Klewan. But before we move on, I'd like to back up a little bit more and ask you, what one thing stands out in your mind the most about being 18 months in that hall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right now, it's almost hard to believe that I lived through it. I would just in my mind question myself, did it really happen? Is it true? How did I do it? I just-- that's why I never talked about it. Because I myself have trouble believing it. And I just can't imagine, can't imagine. The only time really we talked about it is in Israel when we used to get together. And there were all of us there, all eight of us, I would-- should say, there. And we used to talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the thing that the other ones remembered the most?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to make fun and joke about what we talked about, that we used to talk about cooking, and recipes, and the way of life, and things like that that you really would never think that you would talk about those things when you were underground with such a thing. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't special remember. Yes, I remember that myself with my husband at that time, Eli, we, we didn't know about life really much. We didn't have a life special thinking, about going out to the world again, and being married and things like that. But we used to talk about silly things. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] i, I never cooked in my life or didn't know anything about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] living together, or something like that, so silly things at that time, I would say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what other kinds of things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just hoping that we're going to make it and there will still be a world, that it's going to be like we used to know. We didn't-- we didn't expect anything like we know now. But we thought we'll be very happy if we'll have enough bread to eat, or a clean bed or shower, those kind of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who do you think had the hardest time of the eight people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know if there would be other people here, they would say just me and my-- and the cousin, Abraham.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lost my hair. I lost teeth. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] And health-wise-- and also because-- I don't know. I really don't know why, but the others held up better.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think helped you going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we go-- went out?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, when you were still in there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just knowing that there is nothing else to do and being there with my husband at that time, that we were young kids and we were in love. And, and we were planning a life together. We used to talk about it. And we knew that there was nobody left. We knew about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: While we were still alive-- I mean, we were still out there, we knew what's going on. And we heard that there is not Jews anymore in our place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you try and get in touch with your mother after you came out of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. How? You couldn't get a-- there was no telephone or mail. You were afraid for somebody to know where you are.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, after you came out, when you back-- went back to Klewan.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you try and find your mother at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. At that time I knew already exactly that they are gone with all the people that were in Aleksandrja with my-- from my mother's side the family is gone and my sister. But the thing about it is that the Ukrainian people that we lived with knew exactly that-- who is went, who is gone, who is killed, and who should still be around somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was their reaction to this?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came out, they all, most of them said that they knew that the Lippers are somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a good thing or a bad thing for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: If they were-- I don't know what you mean by good or bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have a comment on what they thought about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. In front of you, they told you that you're glad-- they're glad they made it. But probably, they said that Bella should have never done it in their heart, or how did she do it for them without everybody around to her not knowing it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about her family, when they found out?

speaker: interviewee
text: They just couldn't believe it. Her family really didn't know that we were in their house under the floor when we went out. I mean, you-- he knew. We went out during the night that we are alive. But, but they knew that the sister was in touch. They knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about the extra food? Did they notice she was taking extra food?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was raising a pig for that reason, that she should be able to get more potatoes, and flour, and stuff like that. She used to bake bread herself every day in a regular small oven in our apartment. And I wanted to say that she didn't have a house. She had a room in her parents' house. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And she used to-- and the people that sold her the flour and the potatoes that she went to knew something, because they were people that knew us from before. And they knew that she herself didn't have any money, that the money has to come from the Lippers. And-- but they never asked her if she knows something about us, or if she-- if we are in contact with her. They never questioned her. But they told us afterwards that they suspected something. And they helped her, really. And as far as her family, she had an alibi that she raises a pig. And, and that's who she buys the flour and the extra potatoes for. And that was very little. We use to get a loaf of bread every day and just a little soup. That wasn't much, the minimum.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then how long were you in Klewan for?

speaker: interviewee
text: We, we [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] got free in, in '44. And I was in Klewan till May '45. At that time, my daughter was about two months old, two and a half months old. And all the Polish citizens, if they wanted, they could go back to Poland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were Polish citizens. And we were-- we decided to go to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that time, we had a lot of problems with the Russians that we were working with, that we associated with. Why would you want to leave? Where are you going? And we almost had to sneak out to go there.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, we went already by train. And we had to have papers made. And everything was-- had to be done secretly. And in '45, in maybe July, August, we arrived in Krakow, Poland. And that was already organization there from the Jewish society that took us there. And we stayed there a couple months. And then we were going on our way. We wanted to go to Germany, to Austria, to go further, so we can go to Israel or go to America. And also, we had to pay for it. In September of '45, we went with a group. We were Greeks, not-- we were supposed to go through the German, the Russian border, not letting them in any way realize that we are-- understand what they're talking or that we're even Polacks. But we were supposed to say that we're Greeks, and we're going home. We were dis-- displaced by Germans, and now we're going home. And it was very hard thing, but we made it. In fact, in Czechoslovakia, we walked through the border. But we had somebody that led us. It was a organization already, a Jewish organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know. It had something to do with the HIAS. But I really don't know what they call themselves. And in, I would say in the beginning, the end of '45, we were in Vienna. A couple months later, we were sent to Salzburg. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And from there, we found out that my husband's brother with his wife then already, the one, the non-Jewish one, Bella, are in Bad Gastein in Austria. And he came to Salzburg and took us to Bad Gastein. And that's where we lived for about two years-- not two years. It was in '45, about two, two and a half years, until '48, April '48, when we came to this country.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you come to in this country? Where to?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to New York. I got in touch-- a long story. One Pesach, we got matzo from Manischewitz's, Horowitz Margareten matzo, not Manishewitz. And my husband and I said, we're going to write a letter and look for our family in America. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't remember really where they were or something, but I knew the names.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we wrote them a letter. And we gave her that my grandmother is Rachel [NON-ENGLISH] and has an older son, Menachem, and another son, [INAUDIBLE], and a daughter, Rose. And they're in Brooklyn, I know. And I send away the letter. One day, not long, we got a telegram from my uncle, from my father's brother, that who is there, who is left. And I should send them the ages, who's left. And they want us to come to America. And that's how it started. So what did that have to do with the matzo, the matzo?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Horowitz Margareten people called them in the middle of the night and asked them, is your name [? Aaron? ?] He said, yeah. Do you have a mother, Rachel? Do you have a brother, [INAUDIBLE]? Do you have a sister, Rose? Did you have a brother, Menachem. He said, somebody in Europe is looking for you.

speaker: interviewer
text: You sent it to the matzo company, the letter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. And they are the one-- that's how he got my address. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any organizations help you when you came to this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. They get-- sent papers for us. And they brought us over. They are the ones that paid. We weren't under any organization, and so on. And I came to my grandmother's house. And they helped us a little bit. And we went to work, put my daughter in a day nursery. She was three years old. And both of us went to work. And from there, it's history.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I started out in a factory where they made ladies underwear.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nine years later, I had another daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sharon Lipper. Now it's Scheele.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have any grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have two grandchildren from my younger daughter, from Sharon. The first-- my grandson is named after my husband. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Evan. My husband's name was Eli. But I wanted-- didn't want them to name him Eli, because it was at that time when he just passed away. And it was hard for me to hear his name. So the English name is Evan, in Jewish is Eli. He's named after my husband and after my father. So his name is Evan Matthew. And the little girl three years later is named after a aunt of my son in-law's and after my grandmother, Rachel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever speak about your experiences during the war to anyone when you came to this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: My older daughter lived through a lot with us, being in Europe and always with us. And she heard everything what was going on. And I thought that it had a little bit of a reaction on her, that she was different, you know. When I came to this country, I wanted her to become one of the Americans and not to have that burden on her. And we never talked about it, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you talked about it to your children since that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really, not in details, what I'm telling you now, never. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't think that even my husband and I talked about it really in depth when we-- after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I really can't say. It, it's, it's so hard. It's almost hard to believe. And if I was ever approached by people that I knew that I'm a survivor and asked questions, and I always said that I don't want to talk about it. You probably-- to you, it's going to be a story. And for me, I lived through it. And it's hard for me to talk about.

speaker: interviewer
text: So why did you want to tell us your story today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was approached a couple times before. And I always refused. Now, there is nobody left. My sister-in-law in Israel, Bella, the one that deserves all the credit in the world, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] is old lady. All the brothers are gone. Everybody else is gone. I'm the only one left. And I feel that when I'm gone-- I really didn't remember. I never thought that I'll be able to tell you that much what I did. And I thought that I should tell it, that it should be somewhere, should sometimes-- whoever will want to be interested should know about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you could say one thing to future generations who had never met anybody who lived through it, what would you say to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: To never let it happen again, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that it's-- if it's to Jews or to anybody else, never. It shouldn't happen again. Because it just-- it's, it's unbelievable. It's unbelievable what we lived through. And though there is a lot of people that when-- were in concentration camps that maybe went through worse than I did. But-- it just that they shouldn't let it happen again. That's the only thing that I-- The reason that I'm doing it now and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for generations to look at it and say, that it can never happen, that it-- those kind of things should never be acceptable or-- I was a lot of times surprised that a country that I know now of America, that I know so many people, and Jewish people, that they could let it go, that it could happen. It's hard to believe that people didn't know about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit more about your sister-in-law who you said now is an older lady in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: She came to Israel. She was on Cyprus, came to Israel. My brother-in-law was in the Israeli war. She had two children in Israel. Her husband is dead already for about 15 years. That's my brother-in-law. Her daughter of 48 passed away about two years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she ever recognized for what she did during  the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She is in the Golden Book. The older people from our town, that-- when they came to Israel, they knew her. And they knew her husband. They knew about the family. And yes, she has a plaque that she was put in the book of the righteous people, [INAUDIBLE], yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she ever see anybody from the war again?

speaker: interviewee
text: The eight people, we used to get together in Israel. Yes, we were very close. One of my-- the brothers, David and Sonya, lived in Brazil in-- in America. And there are four of them in Israel. But they all gone. She's the only one left in Israel. In Brazil, they're both gone. And I'm the only one in America. So there is the two of us left from the eight people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a story you wanted to tell about the Russian soldier that had--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I don't exactly know what happened, but they got together at some kind of function and sat 16 people by a table. And everybody, where you're from, what did you do, and so on. And one of the survivors with-- that was with us, the single girl, said that where she came from-- and that man said, you know, he says, I was in Klewan. I freed Klewan. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he started to talk about it, that he met a unusual lady there. And she was sitting, not saying anything. But her tears started to come down. And she let him talk. And then she says to him, I know you. You should know me. She says, I'm of the-- one of those eight people. And you can imagine what happened then. And when he heard that she's there, that lady that he met that was not Jewish and all that, he said that he has to see her. And he met with her. At that time, she was already honored about the Golden Book with that. And they got together a couple times. I never got to meet them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said she married your brother's-- your husband's brother. Did she live her life as a Jew then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes. When we were in Austria before she went to Israel and I went to America in Bad Gastein, we both had small children so she used to tell me that if you go down Saturday to the kitchen to warm up the food for the children, it's OK if they'll call you shiksa. But he says, I don't want them to do it to me. Yes, she speaks beautiful Hebrew. Her children are educated. Her daughter was a schoolteacher in Israel. She herself writes Hebrew in beautiful and speaks Hebrew. You would never say that she's not Jewish. And her name is Bella Lipper. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we close, is there anything else about your experience that you'd like to add at this time that I haven't asked?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wish I would have talked to my children a little bit more about it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Sometimes I'm sorry about it, that I didn't talk to them, that they should be more knowledgeable. The only thing they know is that their parents are survivors, but they don't know what we went through. My oldest daughter always used to tell me, how come she hasn't got grandmothers, and aunts, and uncles? And I never really gave a good explanation that she should understand. But I always used to tell them, when you get older, you will understand. That was always my answer. And when they got older, it's too late.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank you very much for sharing your story with us today.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm glad I did it, now that it's over.
